s: The capital of a North African nation

s: The capital of Uganda is Kampala.
q: What is the capital of Uganda?
e: Kampala serves as the national capital and political center. As the capital, it houses the country's government institutions and is typically the largest or most culturally significant city.

e: Kyiv is the capital city of Ukraine.

s: The Cape of Good Hope at the southern tip of Africa was a critical waypoint on the spice trade route from Europe to Asia before the Suez Canal opened.
q: What was the Cape of Good Hope originally called?
e: Portuguese explorer Bartolomeu Dias was the first European to round the Cape in 1488. It was originally called the Cape of Storms due to its treacherous waters.

s: Cape Town, South Africa, is located near the meeting point of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.
q: Near the meeting point of which two oceans is Cape Town located?
e: The cold Benguela Current from the Atlantic and the warm Agulhas Current from the Indian Ocean create dramatically different water temperatures on each side of the Cape Peninsula.

s: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest enclosed body of water, covering about 371,000 square kilometers.
q: What is the world's largest enclosed body of water?
e: Despite being called a sea, the Caspian is technically a lake bordered by five countries: Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan.

s: The Caspian Sea is actually the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers.
q: What is the world's largest lake by surface area?
e: Despite being called a 'sea,' the Caspian is landlocked and has no connection to the ocean. It borders Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan.

s: The capital of Central African Republic is Bangui.
q: What is the capital of Central African Republic?
e: Bangui serves as the vibrant capital of Central African Republic in Africa!

s: Chile is located in the Latin America & Caribbean region.
q: In which World Bank region is Chile located?
e: Chile, with its capital at Santiago, is part of the World Bank's Latin America & Caribbean classification. The country has a population of approximately 19.8 million and is in the Southern and Western Hemispheres.

s: Parts of Chile's Atacama Desert are so barren that NASA uses them as a Mars analogue — some areas have had no recorded rainfall in over 500 years.
q: Which desert is so dry that NASA uses it as a testing ground for Mars equipment?
e: The Atacama is the driest non-polar desert on Earth. Its extreme aridity, UV radiation, and mineral-rich soil closely mimic Martian surface conditions, making it an ideal testing ground for Mars rover technology and astrobiology research.

s: Chile is one of the world's longest north-to-south countries, stretching about 4,300 km but averaging only 177 km wide.
q: Approximately how long is Chile from north to south?
e: Chile's extreme length means it spans many climate zones, from the driest desert (Atacama) in the north to glaciers and fjords in Patagonia.

s: Despite spanning five geographical time zones, China uses only one official time zone (UTC+8, Beijing Time) for the entire country.
q: How many official time zones does China use?
e: This means when it is noon in Beijing on the east coast, it is also officially noon in Kashgar in the far west where the sun may not have risen yet.

s: China built dozens of massive 'ghost cities' — brand new urban districts designed for millions but sitting nearly empty, like Kangbashi in Ordos which was built for 1 million people but initially had only 30,000.
q: Kangbashi in Ordos, built for 1 million people but initially almost empty, exemplifies which Chinese phenomenon?
e: Starting in the 2000s, China's rapid urbanization led to speculative construction of entire cities before populations moved in. Kangbashi in Inner Mongolia became the poster child, though many ghost cities have since partially filled as populations shifted.

s: The capital of Colombia is Bogota.
q: What is the capital of Colombia?
e: Bogota serves as the administrative capital of Colombia, located in the Latin America & Caribbean region. With a population of approximately 52.9 million, Colombia is classified as a upper middle income country by the World Bank.

s: Colombia has more bird species than any other country on Earth — nearly 2,000 — despite being only the 25th-largest country by area.
q: Which country has the most bird species on Earth, with nearly 2,000?
e: Colombia's extraordinary bird diversity results from its unique geography: Andes mountains, Amazon rainforest, Caribbean and Pacific coasts, and páramo grasslands all create distinct habitats. It surpasses even much larger countries like Brazil and Indonesia.

s: Comoros is located in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres.
q: In which hemispheres is Comoros located?
e: Based on its coordinates (-11.6986°, 43.2418°), Comoros sits in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres. Its capital Moroni anchors this lower middle income nation in the Sub-Saharan Africa region.

s: The Congo River is the deepest river in the world, reaching depths of over 220 meters.
q: Which river is the deepest in the world?
e: Located in Central Africa, the Congo's extreme depth is caused by powerful currents carving through bedrock in narrow gorges.

s: Congo, Rep. is located in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres.
q: In which hemispheres is Congo, Rep. located?
e: Based on its coordinates (-4.2767°, 15.2662°), Congo, Rep. sits in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres. Its capital Brazzaville anchors this lower middle income nation in the Sub-Saharan Africa region.

s: The Congo River is the deepest river in the world, reaching depths of over 220 meters in some sections.
q: What is the deepest river in the world?
e: The Congo's extreme depth is caused by powerful currents cutting through narrow gorges, creating underwater canyons that far exceed any other river's depth.

s: The Congo River crosses the equator twice — it is the only major river in the world to do so, looping from the Southern Hemisphere into the Northern and back again.
q: Which is the only major river that crosses the equator twice?
e: The Congo's unusual course traces a massive arc through central Africa. Starting in the highlands of Zambia, it flows north across the equator, curves westward, then crosses the equator again heading south before reaching the Atlantic Ocean.

s: About 335 million years ago, all continents were joined into a single supercontinent called Pangaea.
q: What was the name of the supercontinent that existed about 335 million years ago?
e: Pangaea began to break apart about 200 million years ago. The name comes from Greek meaning 'all lands.' Evidence includes matching fossils and rock formations on now-separated continents.

s: The continental shelf is the submerged extension of a continent, typically extending about 80 km from the coast to a depth of about 200 meters.
q: To what depth does the continental shelf typically extend?
e: Continental shelves cover about 8% of the ocean floor but contain rich fishing grounds, oil and gas deposits, and support most marine biodiversity near the coast.

s: The capital of Costa Rica is San José.
q: What is the capital of Costa Rica?
e: San José serves as the vibrant capital of Costa Rica in North America!

s: Cote d'Ivoire is classified as a lower middle income country by the World Bank.
q: How does the World Bank classify Cote d'Ivoire's income level?
e: The World Bank classifies Cote d'Ivoire as lower middle income, based on gross national income per capita. Cote d'Ivoire's capital is Yamoussoukro, and it is located in the Sub-Saharan Africa region with a population of approximately 31.9 million.

s: Croatia has over 1,200 islands along its Adriatic coast, but only about 50 of them are permanently inhabited.
q: Approximately how many islands does Croatia have?
e: Croatia's Dalmatian coast is one of Europe's most fragmented coastlines. The islands range from tiny uninhabited rocks to sizeable ones like Krk and Cres, each with unique Mediterranean character.

s: Cuba has about 60,000 classic American cars from the 1950s still on the road because a trade embargo prevented new car imports for decades.
q: Why does Cuba have so many classic 1950s American cars?
a: A trade embargo prevented new car imports
d: Colonial Spanish law prohibits vehicle replacement
Soviet Union provided only vintage vehicles
Cars are imported duty-free only from the 1950s
e: When the US imposed its embargo in 1960, Cubans could no longer buy new American vehicles. They kept pre-revolution cars running with improvised parts, creating a living museum of 1950s automobiles.

s: Nicosia, the capital of Cyprus, is the world's last divided capital city — a UN buffer zone called the Green Line has split it between Greek and Turkish Cypriots since 1974.
q: What is the world's last divided capital city?
e: The division dates from Turkey's 1974 military intervention. The buffer zone runs through the old city center, with abandoned buildings frozen in time between the two sides. Some crossing points opened in 2003.

s: The Danakil Depression in Ethiopia is one of the hottest places on Earth, with average annual temperatures exceeding 34°C and surface temperatures reaching 70°C.
q: In which country is the Danakil Depression located?
e: This geological marvel features acid pools, salt pillars, and sulfurous hot springs that create an alien landscape of vivid yellows, greens, and oranges.

s: The Danakil Depression in Ethiopia holds the record for the highest average annual temperature of any inhabited place on Earth, at around 34.4°C (94°F).
q: Which Ethiopian location holds the record for the highest average annual temperature of any inhabited place?
e: Located in the Afar Triangle where three tectonic plates are pulling apart, the Danakil Depression sits well below sea level. Despite brutal heat, sulfuric acid pools, and active volcanoes, the Afar people have lived here for centuries, mining salt by hand.

s: The Danube River flows through ten countries, more than any other river in the world.
q: How many countries does the Danube River flow through?
e: The Danube passes through Germany, Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, and Ukraine on its way to the Black Sea.

s: The Dead Sea, bordering Jordan and Israel, lies approximately 430 meters below sea level, making it the lowest point on Earth's surface.
q: What is the approximate elevation of the Dead Sea below sea level?
e: The extreme salinity of the Dead Sea — nearly 10 times saltier than the ocean — allows swimmers to float effortlessly on its surface.

s: The Dead Sea is so salty (about 34% salinity) that you literally cannot sink in it — the water is nearly 10 times saltier than the ocean and dense enough to make any human body float effortlessly.
q: At about 34% salinity, which body of water is so dense that humans float in it effortlessly?
e: Located 430 m below sea level between Israel, Jordan, and the West Bank, the Dead Sea's extreme salinity comes from mineral-rich water flowing in with no outlet. The water's density is about 1.24 kg/L compared to 1.025 kg/L for normal seawater.
